title: Fractional short-time dynamics in driven quantum gases
abstract: Quantum gases with short-range attractive interaction have a tendency to form pairs. For time-dependent interaction we find that the pairing amplitude at small separation satisfies a fractional differential equation (FDE). We derive analytic solutions of the pairing evolution for sudden interaction quenches and power-law drives toward resonant scattering. We observe universal short-time dynamics governed by a conformal fixed point at which the momentum distribution exhibits nonthermal, self-similar scaling in time, in quantitative agreement with experiment. At longer times, many-body effects induce relaxation toward an equilibrium state. In this limit, the FDE turns into a Müller-Israel-Stewart type equation that describes a hydrodynamic attractor approaching equilibrium.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the short-time evolution of pairing in driven quantum gases is governed by a fractional differential equation, leading to universal non-thermal scaling. It further shows that as many-body effects take over, the dynamics transition into a hydrodynamic regime described by a Müller-Israel-Stewart type equation. The theoretical predictions show excellent agreement with experimental observations of momentum distributions.
main_problem: Connecting the early-time, non-universal dynamics of driven quantum gases to their late-time hydrodynamic equilibrium regimes.
main_result: The authors discover that short-time pairing dynamics satisfy a fractional differential equation (FDE) characterized by a conformal fixed point and non-thermal scaling, which eventually relaxes into a Müller-Israel-Stewart type hydrodynamic attractor.
method: The study employs Riemann-Liouville fractional calculus, Laplace transforms, and the Lippmann-Schwinger equation to derive analytical solutions for sudden quenches and power-law drives.
model_or_system: The system consists of dilute, ultracold atomic gases with short-range attractive interactions, modeled by a time-dependent Schrödinger equation with a delta-function potential and Bethe-Peierls boundary conditions.
key_observables: Pairing amplitude, contact density, momentum distribution, and particle population growth.
important_parameters: Scattering length a(t), diffusion quantum D0, relaxation time tau, and non-thermal scaling exponents alpha and beta.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the pairing amplitude is differentiable and that the initial integral of the fractional derivative is zero; it also assumes a specific form for the power-law drive.
figures_summary: The notes refer to a figure showing the theoretical prediction for particle population growth matching experimental data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of early-to-late time dynamics, derives the fractional differential equation for short-time pairing, provides analytical solutions for various driving protocols (quenches and power-laws), and concludes by describing the transition to the hydrodynamic regime and comparison with experiment.
why_interesting: It provides a unified mathematical framework (fractional calculus) to bridge the gap between non-equilibrium, non-thermal fixed points and classical hydrodynamics in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 3. Momentum distribution n(k, t) vs. momentum after a quench to the scattering resonance at (dimensionless) times ¯t = D0k2 ∗t = 0.2 (black) and ¯t = 0.8 (orange), where the ref- erence scale is defined through the characteristic momentum k3 ∗= 6π2n in analogy with the Fermi momentum. One ob- serves nonthermal scaling with a unique scaling function fs (18), which at the later time (orange) is shifted left by a factor 2 (β = 1/2) and up by a factor 64 (α = 3). The coefficient of the k−4 tail (19) defines the contact density C(t).

FIG. 4. Experimental data showing the normalized change in the momentum distribution due to interaction from [16]. The collapse of different k curves confirms the scaling form (18). The full black line is the theoretical prediction computed at T/TF = 0.26, k/kF = 1.26 and τ/τF = 0.9 from Eq. (35).

FIG. 5. Dynamics of the pairing amplitude η for a power-law drive of scattering length a(t) = −t2, with D0 = 10−3, τ = 20. The black curve shows the solution of the tempered FDE (20) obtained with a numerical FDE solver [36–38]. The different asymptotic scaling forms η →min(a(t), 2 √π √D0t, √D0τ) are shown by the yellow, blue, and green dashed lines, respec- tively.
